content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 24, 1989

Honorable Ross Johnson
Assembly Member, Sixty-Fourth District
State Capitol
P.O. Box 942849
Sacramento, CA 94249-0001

Re: Our File No. G-89-237

Dear Assemblyman Johnson:

This letter is in response to your letter of March 14, 1989, concerning the Orange County Register article concerning Assembly Speaker Willie Brown's contributions to support a lawsuit against Assemblyman Curt Pringle. We have checked our advice records and we have no record of advising Mr. Brown on this subject. As you are aware, we cannot advise you concerning Mr. Brown's obligations under the Political Reform Act unless he has authorized you to request that advice. (Regulation 18329(b)(8), copy enclosed.)

If you wish us to treat your letter as a complaint, to be referred to the Commission's Enforcement Division, please notify me as soon as possible.

Please contact me at (916) 322-5901 if you have any questions about this letter.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

SACRAMENTO OFFICE
STATE CAPITOL
P.O. BOX 942849
SACRAMENTO, CA 94249-0001
(916) 445-7448

DISTRICT OFFICE
1501 NORTH HARBOR BOULEVARD
SUITE 201
FULLERTON, CA 92635
(714) 736-5853
(213) 697-0603

Assembly
California Legislature

ROSS JOHNSON
ASSEMBLYMAN, SIXTY-FOURTH DISTRICT

ASSEMBLY REPUBLICAN LEADER

March 14, 1989

Mr. John Larson
Chairman
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear John:

A recent news article in the Orange County Register indicated that
Assembly Speaker Willie Brown has contributed $50,000 from his
campaign funds to support a lawsuit against Assemblyman Curt
Pringle.

Since your staff advises me that restricted funds can be used only
for paying off a particular candidate's campaign debts or
officeholder expenses, as defined in Regulation 18536.2, and since
clearly 1990 funds cannot be used, I am confused as to how Mr.
Brown was able to make the contribution.

If there is any rationale consistent with the advice being given
by your staff which would allow such an expenditure by Speaker
Brown, I would like to be informed of that rationale.

I would appreciate a reply at your earliest convenience.

Sincerely,

ROSS JOHNSON
Assembly Republican Leader

RJ:co

Monday, March 6, 1989    The Orange County Register

Democratic campaign consultant
considers switch to GOP

OC POLITICAL
NOTES

By Larry Peterson
The Register

Harvey Englander, a veteran
Orange County Democratic cam-
paign consultant, said he is think-
ing seriously about becoming a Re-
publican.

Englander has been running
campaigns for more than a de-
cade, including those of four Or-
ange County supervisors, Los An-
geles City Councilman Michael
Woo and Proposition 101, the un-
successful 1988 auto insurance bal-
lot measure.

The Newport Beach-based strat-
egist's misgivings are not recent.
He has been increasingly critical
over the past three years of state
and local Democratic officials and
what he sees as their leftward bent.

"I'm concerned about the future
ability of the Democratic Party to
reflect the views of moderates,"
said Englander, who describes
himself as one. "I'm concerned
that the party of Willie Brown, Ron
Brown and Jerry Brown is no long-
er my party."

He was referring, respectively,
to the speaker of the state Assem-
bly, the chairman of the Democratic National Committee and the for-

Reagan Democrats abound

Former state Democratic Chair-
man Richard O'Neill of San Juan
Capistrano defends Willie Brown's
advice that the party should write
off the Reagan Democrats.

"Most of them already have con-
sidered themselves Republicans
for years," he said. "They just nev-
er got around to re-registering. We
have a lot of them in Orange Coun-
ty in places like Garden Grove and
Westminster."

O'Neill said that might be one
reason that partisan breakdowns
reflected in statewide voter regis-
tration statistics differ sharply
from the results when pollsters ask
voters which party they prefer.

The former reflected a substantial
Democratic advantage; a latter
show the GOP ahead even with the
Democrats.

But aren't "Reagan Democrats"
prompted to re-register Republican
when they are handed primary
election ballots that offer them
choices only among Democrats in
partisan races?

"Most of them don't vote in pri-
maries," O'Neill said.

Brown opposes recall vote

Although Willie Brown has not
supported the effort by local Dem-
ocrats to mount a recall election
against freshman Assemblyman

Harvey Englander
Decision is philosophical, economic

[illegible]

72-337

JUN 24 5 OR 1* 88

June 20, 1988

Legal Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804

To Whom It May Concern:

I am a member of the Yolo County Mental Health Advisory Board
(MHAB), and also a member of the Board of the Yolo Community Care
Continuum (YCCC), a non-profit (Short-Doyle) contract agency. I
am also a consumer of YCCC. I am not an employee of YCCC, nor do
I have any business interest in YCCC.

I would like to know if there are specific current conflict of
interest regulations governing my participation on the Yolo
County MHAB.

Thank you for any assistance you can provide.

Sincerely,

Marcia Livingstone
160 Third Street
Woodland, CA 95695

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission


June 27, 1988


Marcia Livingstone
160 Third Street
Woodland, CA  95695

Re:  88-237

Dear Ms. Livingstone:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform
Act was received on June 24, 1988 by the Fair Political
Practices Commission.  If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact Margarita Altamirano, an
attorney in the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions,
or more information is needed, you should expect a response
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written
advice.  If more information is needed, the person assigned to
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to
advise you as to information needed.  If your request is for
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can.
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec.
18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,


Diane M. Griffiths
General Counsel

428 I Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 827 • Sacramento, CA 95814-0827

sections.question_synthetic: Assemblyman Johnson inquired whether Assembly Speaker Willie Brown could legally contribute $50,000 from his campaign funds to support a lawsuit against another assemblyman, given restrictions on use of campaign funds for candidate debts or officeholder expenses.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC declined to provide advice on Mr. Brown's obligations under the Political Reform Act because he had not authorized the request and the FPPC had no prior advice records on this subject. The FPPC offered to treat the inquiry as a complaint to be referred to the Enforcement Division if requested.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains multiple items: incoming request from Assemblyman Johnson (March 14, 1989), FPPC response (April 24, 1989), and unrelated newspaper article and earlier receipt letter (1988). Primary analysis focuses on the April 1989 FPPC advice response letter. No formal Q/C sections present; information extracted from body of correspondence.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Assemblyman Johnson inquired whether Assembly Speaker Willie Brown could legally contribute $50,000 from his campaign funds to support a lawsuit against another assemblyman, given restrictions on use of campaign funds for candidate debts or officeholder expenses.

CONCLUSION: The FPPC declined to provide advice on Mr. Brown's obligations under the Political Reform Act because he had not authorized the request and the FPPC had no prior advice records on this subject. The FPPC offered to treat the inquiry as a complaint to be referred to the Enforcement Division if requested.

embedding.summary: An FPPC General Counsel response letter declining to advise on Assembly Speaker Willie Brown's legal obligations regarding a campaign contribution to a lawsuit, noting that Brown had not authorized the advice request.
